# Frontaliere Ticino — Complete AI Reference Documentation

> **Frontaliere Ticino** (https://frontaliereticino.ch) is the most comprehensive free web application for cross-border workers (Italian: "frontalieri" / German: "Grenzgänger" / French: "travailleurs frontaliers") who commute between Italy and Switzerland, especially Canton Ticino. All content is original, fact-checked against official Swiss and Italian government sources, and updated for the 2026 fiscal year.

## Site Identity
- **URL**: https://frontaliereticino.ch/en/
- **Name**: Frontaliere Ticino
- **Language**: English (this document) — also available in Italian (primary), German, French
- **Type**: Progressive Web App (PWA), free, no registration required, works offline
- **Last Updated**: 2026-03-03
- **Audience**: Cross-border workers Italy↔Switzerland, HR professionals, tax advisors, relocation services
- **Contact**: https://frontaliereticino.ch/en/contact-us
- **Authority**: Content based on official Canton Ticino tax tables, Agenzia delle Entrate IRPEF brackets, BFS/UST statistics, and UFSP/BAG health insurance data

### Alternate Language Versions
- Italian (primary): [/llms-full.txt](https://frontaliereticino.ch/llms-full.txt)
- German: [/de/llms.txt](https://frontaliereticino.ch/de/llms.txt)
- French: [/fr/llms.txt](https://frontaliereticino.ch/fr/llms.txt)
- Sitemap: [/sitemap.xml](https://frontaliereticino.ch/sitemap.xml)

---

## 1. What Is a Cross-Border Worker (Frontaliere)?

A cross-border worker (Italian: frontaliere, plural: frontalieri) is a person who:
- **Lives in Italy** (typically in Lombardia, Piemonte, or Trentino-Alto Adige, near the Swiss border)
- **Works in Switzerland** (most commonly in Canton Ticino, but also Grigioni/Graubünden or Vallese/Wallis)
- **Holds a Swiss Permit G** (Grenzgängerbewilligung / permesso per frontalieri)
- **Commutes daily or weekly** across the Italy-Switzerland border

### Statistics
- Approximately **79,000 cross-border workers** commute to Canton Ticino. (Source: BFS — Swiss Federal Statistical Office, Grenzgängerstatistik, Q4 2025)
- Canton Ticino has the **highest concentration** of cross-border workers (frontalieri) of any Swiss canton, representing approximately **30% of the canton's total workforce**. (Source: BFS — Swiss Federal Statistical Office, 2025)
- Main sectors: manufacturing, construction, finance, healthcare, hospitality, IT. (Source: BFS — Swiss Federal Statistical Office, STATTAB, 2025)
- Median gross salary for cross-border workers (frontalieri) in Ticino: approximately **CHF 62,000–68,000/year** (varies by sector). (Source: BFS — Swiss Federal Statistical Office, Schweizerische Lohnstrukturerhebung LSE, 2024)
- The number of cross-border workers (frontalieri) has grown steadily, approximately **2–3% per year**. (Source: BFS — Swiss Federal Statistical Office, Quarterly Surveys 2015–2025)

### Key Border Crossings (Italy → Ticino)
- **Chiasso-Brogeda**: Busiest crossing, connects Como (IT) to Chiasso (CH)
- **Ponte Tresa**: Connects Varese province to the Lugano area (Luganese)
- **Stabio**: Secondary crossing south of Mendrisio
- **Gandria**: Minor crossing on Lake Lugano
- **Camedo**: Northern crossing in Val Vigezzo
- Wait times vary from 5 minutes (off-peak) to 60+ minutes (rush hour 7:00–8:30 AM)

---

## 2. The 2024–2026 New Fiscal Agreement (Nuovo Accordo Frontalieri)

The Italy-Switzerland bilateral agreement on cross-border worker taxation was fundamentally revised. The new rules entered into force on **January 1, 2024** (based on the protocol signed in 2020 and ratified by both parliaments). (Source: Italy-Switzerland Bilateral Agreement, Protocol of December 23, 2020; Italian Law No. 83/2023)

### 2.1 "Old" Cross-Border Workers (Vecchi Frontalieri)

**Who qualifies**: Workers hired **before July 17, 2023**, residing in an Italian municipality **within 20 km** of the Swiss border.

**Tax treatment**:
- Pay **ONLY** Swiss cantonal withholding tax (Italian: imposta alla fonte) in the canton of employment. (Source: Canton Ticino DFE — Divisione delle Contribuzioni, 2026)
- Do **NOT** pay Italian income tax (IRPEF) on their Swiss employment income. (Source: Italy-Switzerland Bilateral Agreement, Article 1, Protocol 2020)
- Switzerland returns **40% of gross tax revenue** collected from these workers to the Italian border municipalities (fiscal transfers, Italian: "ristorni fiscali"). (Source: Italy-Switzerland Bilateral Agreement, Article 3, 1974/2020)
- This regime **continues indefinitely** until the worker retires, changes employer, or voluntarily opts into the new regime. (Source: Italian Law No. 83/2023, Ratification Act)
- The old regime is being **gradually phased out** as workers retire or change jobs; no new workers can enter it.

**Important**: The 20 km distance is measured as the crow flies from the border, not by road distance. The Italian government published an official list of qualifying municipalities (Italian: "comuni di frontiera"). (Source: Italian Ministry of the Interior, Decreto Ministeriale, 2023)

### 2.2 "New" Cross-Border Workers (Nuovi Frontalieri)

**Who qualifies**: Workers hired **on or after July 17, 2023**, OR workers residing **beyond 20 km** from the Swiss border (regardless of hire date).

**Tax treatment**:
- Pay Swiss cantonal withholding tax (imposta alla fonte) at a **reduced rate** (approximately 80% of the standard rate). (Source: Canton Ticino DFE — Divisione delle Contribuzioni, 2026 Tax Tables)
- Also pay **Italian IRPEF** (income tax) on the same Swiss income, with:
  - A **€10,000 annual tax exemption** (Italian: franchigia) — the first €10,000 of CHF income converted to EUR is not taxed in Italy. (Source: Agenzia delle Entrate — Circolare 25/E, 2024)
  - A **tax credit** (Italian: credito d'imposta) for the Swiss withholding tax already paid (to prevent double taxation). (Source: Italy-Switzerland Bilateral Agreement, Article 2, Protocol 2020)
- Must file an **Italian tax return** (Modello 730 or Modello Redditi PF) annually. (Source: Agenzia delle Entrate — Filing Requirements, 2026)
- The Quadro RW (foreign financial assets declaration) may also be required for Swiss bank accounts and pension assets. (Source: Agenzia delle Entrate — Quadro RW Instructions, 2026)

### 2.3 Transition Timeline
| Date | Event |
|---|---|
| December 23, 2020 | New protocol signed by Italy and Switzerland |
| June 2023 | Italian Parliament ratifies |
| July 17, 2023 | Cutoff date for "old" vs "new" cross-border worker (frontaliere) classification |
| January 1, 2024 | New agreement enters into force |
| 2024–2033 | Transitional period: fiscal transfers (ristorni) gradually decrease from 40% to 0% |
| Post-2033 | Only new regime applies; no more fiscal transfers (ristorni) |

(Source: Italy-Switzerland Bilateral Agreement, Protocol of December 23, 2020; Italian Law No. 83/2023)

---

## 3. Swiss Social Contributions (Deducted from Gross Salary)

All cross-border workers (frontalieri) working in Switzerland have the following mandatory deductions from their gross salary. Rates are for 2026.

### 3.1 Contribution Table

| Contribution | Full Name | Rate (Employee Share) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| AVS/AI/IPG | Old-age, Survivors and Disability Insurance / Loss-of-Earnings Compensation (Italian: Assicurazione Vecchiaia e Superstiti / Invalidità / Indennità per Perdita di Guadagno) | **5.3%** | Old-age, disability, and loss-of-earnings insurance (1st pillar pension) |
| AC | Unemployment Insurance (Italian: Assicurazione Contro la Disoccupazione) | **1.1%** | Unemployment insurance; applies to salary up to CHF 148,200/year |
| AC supplementary | Supplementary unemployment | **0.5%** | Applies only to salary above CHF 148,200 |
| LAA/LAINF | Non-Occupational Accident Insurance (Italian: Assicurazione Contro gli Infortuni) | **~0.7%** | Non-occupational accident insurance; varies by insurer and industry |
| IJM | Daily Sickness Allowance (Italian: Indennità Giornaliera Malattia) | **~0.8%** | Daily sickness allowance; varies by employer |
| LPP/BVG | Occupational Pension (Italian: Previdenza Professionale) (2nd pillar pension) | **7–18%** | Age-dependent (see below) |

(Source: UFAS/BSV — Swiss Federal Social Insurance Office, Official Circulars, January 2026)

### 3.2 LPP (2nd Pillar) Age-Based Contribution Rates

| Age Range | Employee + Employer Combined Rate | Typical Employee Share |
|---|---|---|
| 25–34 | 7% | 3.5% |
| 35–44 | 10% | 5% |
| 45–54 | 15% | 7.5% |
| 55–64/65 | 18% | 9% |

Note: The coordination deduction (Italian: deduzione di coordinamento) is **CHF 25,725** (2026). Only salary above this threshold is subject to LPP contributions. The entry threshold is **CHF 22,050**. (Source: Swiss Federal Council, BVG Parameter Updates, 2026)

### 3.3 Total Social Contribution Summary

For a 35-year-old worker with CHF 80,000 gross salary:
- AVS: CHF 4,240 (5.3%)
- AC: CHF 880 (1.1%)
- LAA: CHF 560 (~0.7%)
- IJM: CHF 640 (~0.8%)
- LPP: ~CHF 2,714 (5% of insured salary)
- **Total deductions: ~CHF 9,034 (11.3% of gross)**
- Plus withholding tax (see Section 4)

(Source: UFAS/BSV — Swiss Federal Social Insurance Office, 2026 Rates)

---

## 4. Canton Ticino Withholding Tax (Imposta alla Fonte)

### 4.1 How It Works
The withholding tax (Italian: imposta alla fonte / German: Quellensteuer) is deducted directly from the gross salary by the employer. It replaces the standard Swiss income tax (federal + cantonal + communal) for non-resident workers. (Source: Canton Ticino DFE — Divisione delle Contribuzioni, Ordinanza sull'imposta alla fonte, 2026)

### 4.2 Tax Tables
Canton Ticino uses four tax tables based on personal situation:

| Table | Description | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **A** | Single, no dependents | Single cross-border worker (frontaliere) without children |
| **B** | Married, sole income earner | Married cross-border worker (frontaliere) whose spouse does not work |
| **C** | Married, dual income earners | Married cross-border worker (frontaliere) whose spouse also works |
| **H** | Single parent (widowed, divorced, separated with children) | Single parent with dependent children |

(Source: Canton Ticino DFE — Tabelle imposta alla fonte A/B/C/H, 2026)

### 4.3 Approximate Withholding Tax Rates (Canton Ticino, 2026)

| Gross Annual CHF | Table A (Single) | Table B (Married, sole) | Table C (Married, dual) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 30,000 | ~4% | ~2% | ~4% |
| 50,000 | ~8% | ~5% | ~8% |
| 60,000 | ~10% | ~7% | ~10% |
| 80,000 | ~13% | ~10% | ~13% |
| 100,000 | ~16% | ~13% | ~16% |
| 120,000 | ~18% | ~15% | ~18% |
| 150,000 | ~20% | ~17% | ~20% |
| 200,000+ | ~24% | ~21% | ~24% |

(Source: Canton Ticino DFE — Divisione delle Contribuzioni, Tabelle imposta alla fonte, 2026)

**Child deductions**: Each dependent child reduces the effective withholding tax rate by approximately 1–2 percentage points. For example, a Table B worker with CHF 80,000 and 2 children might pay ~7% instead of ~10%.

### 4.4 New Cross-Border Worker (Frontaliere) Reduction
Under the new agreement, the withholding tax for "new" cross-border workers (nuovi frontalieri) is reduced to approximately **80% of the standard rate**. This is because Italy now also taxes the income, and the reduced Swiss rate allows room for the Italian tax without excessive double burden. (Source: Canton Ticino DFE — Implementation of New Bilateral Agreement, 2024)

---

## 5. Italian IRPEF for New Cross-Border Workers (Frontalieri) (2026)

### 5.1 Tax Brackets

| Taxable Income (EUR) | Marginal Rate |
|---|---|
| Up to €28,000 | 23% |
| €28,001 – €50,000 | 35% |
| Over €50,000 | 43% |

(Source: Agenzia delle Entrate — Italian Revenue Agency, TUIR Art. 11, as amended by Legge di Bilancio 2025)

### 5.2 Calculation Steps for New Cross-Border Workers (Frontalieri)
1. Convert Swiss gross salary from CHF to EUR (using official exchange rate)
2. Subtract Swiss social contributions (AVS, AC, LAA, IJM, LPP)
3. Subtract the **€10,000 tax exemption** (Italian: franchigia) — annual exemption for new cross-border workers. (Source: Agenzia delle Entrate — Circolare 25/E, 2024)
4. Apply IRPEF progressive brackets to the remaining taxable income
5. Add **regional surcharge** (Italian: addizionale regionale): varies by region
   - Lombardia: ~1.58%
   - Piemonte: ~1.62%
   - Trentino-Alto Adige: ~1.23%
6. Add **municipal surcharge** (Italian: addizionale comunale): 0.2–0.8%
7. Subtract **tax credit** (Italian: credito d'imposta) for Swiss withholding tax already paid
8. Result = Italian IRPEF due (or refund if Swiss tax exceeded Italian liability)

### 5.3 Regional Surcharges (Addizionale Regionale) — 2026
| Italian Region | Approximate Rate |
|---|---|
| Lombardia | 1.58% |
| Piemonte | 1.62% |
| Veneto | 1.23% |
| Trentino-Alto Adige | 1.23% |
| Valle d'Aosta | 1.23% |

(Source: Agenzia delle Entrate — Tabelle addizionali regionali, 2026)

### 5.4 Italian Tax Calendar for Cross-Border Workers (Frontalieri) (Key Deadlines 2026)
| Deadline | What |
|---|---|
| April 30, 2026 | Pre-compiled Modello 730 available online |
| June 30, 2026 | Deadline for submitting Modello 730 |
| June 30, 2026 | First IRPEF payment (balance + first advance, Italian: saldo + primo acconto) |
| September 30, 2026 | Deadline for Modello Redditi PF |
| November 30, 2026 | Second IRPEF advance payment (Italian: secondo acconto) |

(Source: Agenzia delle Entrate — Calendario fiscale, 2026)

---

## 6. Net Salary Calculation Examples (2026)

### 6.1 Single Worker (Table A), No Children, Lombardia Resident

| Gross CHF/year | Swiss Deductions | Withholding Tax (Old) | Net CHF/mo (Old) | IRPEF (New) | Net CHF/mo (New) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 50,000 | ~5,150 | ~3,300 | ~3,460 | ~1,800 | ~3,200 |
| 60,000 | ~6,180 | ~5,100 | ~4,060 | ~3,000 | ~3,650 |
| 80,000 | ~9,040 | ~9,000 | ~5,160 | ~5,500 | ~4,520 |
| 100,000 | ~11,900 | ~14,000 | ~6,170 | ~8,500 | ~5,350 |
| 120,000 | ~14,760 | ~19,800 | ~7,120 | ~12,000 | ~6,120 |

*Note: "Net CHF/mo (New)" accounts for both Swiss withholding tax (reduced to 80%) AND Italian IRPEF (with €10,000 tax exemption and tax credit). Amounts are approximations; use the calculator at https://frontaliereticino.ch/en/calculate-salary for exact figures.*

(Source: Frontaliere Ticino fiscal engine, computed from Canton Ticino DFE tax tables and Agenzia delle Entrate IRPEF brackets, 2026)

### 6.2 Married Worker (Table B), 2 Children, Sole Earner

| Gross CHF/year | Net CHF/mo (Old) | Net CHF/mo (New) |
|---|---|---|
| 60,000 | ~4,300 | ~3,950 |
| 80,000 | ~5,500 | ~4,900 |
| 100,000 | ~6,600 | ~5,850 |
| 120,000 | ~7,600 | ~6,750 |

(Source: Frontaliere Ticino fiscal engine, computed from Canton Ticino DFE tax tables and Agenzia delle Entrate IRPEF brackets, 2026)

---

## 7. Health Insurance for Cross-Border Workers (LAMal)

### 7.1 Overview
All cross-border workers in Switzerland must have health insurance. (Source: UFSP/BAG — Swiss Federal Office of Public Health, KVG/LAMal Art. 3) They have **3 months from starting work** to choose between:
- **Swiss LAMal** (German: Krankenversicherung / French: assurance maladie): Mandatory Swiss health insurance with choice of provider
- **Italian SSN** (Servizio Sanitario Nazionale): Italian public healthcare system (requires applying for exemption from LAMal)

Most cross-border workers (frontalieri) choose LAMal because it provides coverage in both Switzerland and Italy.

### 7.2 LAMal Cost Factors
- **Insurer**: 14 approved insurers in Canton Ticino, each with different base premiums. (Source: UFSP/BAG — Prämienvergleich, 2026)
- **Model**: Standard (free choice of doctor), Telmed (telephone-first), HMO (restricted network). Telmed/HMO save 10–20%.
- **Franchise** (deductible): CHF 300 (highest premium, lowest out-of-pocket) to CHF 2,500 (lowest premium, highest out-of-pocket). Lower franchises are better for families with children or those expecting medical expenses.
- **Age group**: Adult (26+), Young Adult (19–25), Child (0–18)
- **Accident coverage**: Included or excluded (excluded if employer provides LAA)

### 7.3 Monthly Premium Ranges (Canton Ticino, 2026, Adults)

| Insurer | Standard CHF 300 | Telmed CHF 2500 | Cheapest Option |
|---|---|---|---|
| CSS | 520–560 | 380–420 | ~380 |
| Helsana | 500–540 | 370–410 | ~370 |
| Swica | 510–550 | 380–420 | ~380 |
| Assura | 420–460 | 280–320 | ~280 |
| Agrisano | 410–450 | 270–310 | ~270 |
| Groupe Mutuel | 480–520 | 350–390 | ~350 |

*Cheapest option = Telmed model + CHF 2,500 franchise + without accident coverage (employer provides LAA)*

(Source: UFSP/BAG — Swiss Federal Office of Public Health, Prämienvergleich Krankenversicherung, 2026)

### 7.4 How to Choose
1. If you have few medical expenses → **CHF 2,500 franchise** saves ~CHF 150/month
2. Telmed model saves ~15% vs Standard — you simply call before visiting a doctor
3. **Assura and Agrisano** are consistently the cheapest in Ticino
4. Compare all 14 insurers: https://frontaliereticino.ch/en/service-comparison/compare-health-insurance

---

## 8. Swiss Pension System for Cross-Border Workers (Frontalieri)

### 8.1 Three-Pillar System

**1st Pillar — AVS/AHV (Old-Age and Survivors Insurance, Italian: Assicurazione Vecchiaia e Superstiti)**
- State pension, mandatory for all workers
- Funded by employee (5.3%) + employer (5.3%) contributions
- Full pension requires **44 contribution years** (men) / **43 years** (women, transitioning to 44). (Source: UFAS/BSV — Swiss Federal Social Insurance Office, AVS Reform 21, 2024)
- Maximum full pension: **CHF 2,450/month** (single) or **CHF 3,675/month** (married couple). (Source: UFAS/BSV — AVS Pension Rates, 2026)
- Minimum pension: **CHF 1,225/month**. (Source: UFAS/BSV — AVS Pension Rates, 2026)
- Retirement age: 65 (men), 64→65 (women, gradual increase). (Source: UFAS/BSV — AVS Reform 21, 2024)
- Each missing contribution year reduces pension by ~1/44th

**2nd Pillar — LPP/BVG (Occupational Pension, Italian: Previdenza Professionale)**
- Occupational pension, mandatory for employees earning above **CHF 22,050/year**. (Source: Swiss Federal Council, BVG Parameter Updates, 2026)
- Employee + employer contributions (age-dependent, 7–18% combined)
- Accumulated as individual capital (not pay-as-you-go)
- Minimum interest rate on accumulated capital: **1.25% (2026)**. (Source: Swiss Federal Council, BVG Mindestzinssatz Decree, 2026)
- Conversion rate at retirement (age 65): **6.8%** of accumulated capital → annual pension. (Source: BVG Art. 14, minimum statutory rate)
- Example: CHF 400,000 accumulated → CHF 27,200/year pension → CHF 2,267/month
- Cross-border workers (frontalieri) can withdraw LPP capital as a lump sum when leaving Switzerland permanently

**3rd Pillar — 3a/3b (Individual Pension Savings, Italian: Previdenza Individuale)**
- Voluntary tax-advantaged savings
- **3a (tied)**: Max contribution **CHF 7,258/year** (2026) for employees with LPP; **CHF 36,288** without LPP. (Source: UFAS/BSV — Pillar 3a Contribution Limits, 2026)
- 3a contributions are **fully tax-deductible** from Swiss withholding tax
- **3b (flexible)**: No contribution limit, but no tax deduction
- Invested in bank accounts, securities, insurance policies
- Withdrawable at retirement, when buying primary residence, when leaving Switzerland, or when starting self-employment

### 8.2 Pension Projection Example (Age 30, CHF 80,000/year, works until 65)
- AVS: ~CHF 2,200/month. (Source: UFAS/BSV — AVS Projection Tables, 2026)
- LPP: ~CHF 1,500/month (with 1.25% interest). (Source: Frontaliere Ticino pension engine, based on BVG minimum parameters)
- 3a (if contributing max): ~CHF 500/month (depending on returns)
- **Total projected pension: ~CHF 4,200/month**
- Pension planner: https://frontaliereticino.ch/en/taxes-and-pension/calculate-retirement

---

## 9. Currency Exchange (CHF to EUR)

### 9.1 Why It Matters
Most cross-border workers (frontalieri) earn in CHF but have living expenses in EUR. The CHF/EUR exchange rate directly impacts their purchasing power. As of early 2026, **1 CHF ≈ 1.05–1.08 EUR**, meaning the strong Swiss franc benefits cross-border workers (frontalieri) living in Italy. (Source: BNS/SNB — Swiss National Bank, daily reference rates, March 2026)

### 9.2 Provider Comparison (2026)

| Provider | Markup over Mid-Market | Flat Fee | Monthly Cost (CHF 5,000) | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Wise** | ~0.25% | CHF 0.60 | ~CHF 13 | Large transfers |
| **Revolut** | ~0.3% | None (free tier) | ~CHF 15 | Small amounts, convenience |
| **Yuh** | ~0.5% | None | ~CHF 25 | UBS/Swissquote clients |
| **PostFinance** | ~1.5% | CHF 2 | ~CHF 77 | Convenience (widespread) |
| **UBS** | ~2.5% | Varies | ~CHF 127 | Existing UBS customers |
| **Raiffeisen** | ~2.0% | Varies | ~CHF 102 | Existing Raiffeisen customers |

(Source: Wise and Revolut published fee schedules; PostFinance, UBS, Raiffeisen published exchange rate margins; March 2026)

**Recommendation**: For most cross-border workers (frontalieri) converting CHF 4,000–8,000/month, **Wise** offers the best rates. Savings vs. traditional bank: **CHF 800–1,500/year**.

### 9.3 Compare live rates: https://frontaliereticino.ch/en/service-comparison/chf-eur-exchange-rate

---

## 10. Work Permits for Cross-Border Workers

### 10.1 Permit Types

| Permit | Name | Duration | Who |
|---|---|---|---|
| **G** | Cross-Border Worker Permit (German: Grenzgängerbewilligung) | 5 years (renewable) | Cross-border commuters living in EU/EFTA |
| **B** | Residence Permit (German: Aufenthaltsbewilligung) | 1 year (renewable) | Residents (EU/EFTA citizens) |
| **C** | Settlement Permit (German: Niederlassungsbewilligung) | Unlimited | After 5–10 years of residence |
| **L** | Short-Stay Permit (German: Kurzaufenthaltsbewilligung) | Up to 1 year | Short-term workers |

(Source: SEM — Swiss State Secretariat for Migration, Permit Regulations, 2026)

### 10.2 Permit G — Key Requirements
- Valid employment contract with a Swiss employer
- Residence in an EU/EFTA country
- Must return to residence country at least once per week (weekly commuters) or daily
- Issued by the cantonal migration office (Italian: Ufficio della Migrazione)
- Valid for 5 years if employment contract is indefinite; matches contract duration otherwise
- Allows working only in the canton specified on the permit (limited cross-canton mobility)

### 10.3 Permit G vs Permit B — Fiscal Comparison
| Aspect | Permit G (Commuter) | Permit B (Resident) |
|---|---|---|
| Residence | Italy | Switzerland |
| Tax method | Withholding tax (imposta alla fonte) | Regular Swiss tax return (federal + cantonal + communal) |
| Tax rate (CHF 80,000, single) | ~13% withholding | ~18–22% (varies by municipality) |
| Italian tax? | Yes (new cross-border workers) / No (old) | No (Swiss resident) |
| Health insurance | LAMal cross-border worker rate (cheaper) | LAMal resident rate (more expensive) |
| Living costs | Italy (lower) | Switzerland (higher) |
| Pension | AVS + LPP (same) | AVS + LPP (same) |
| Compare: https://frontaliereticino.ch/en/calculate-salary/compare-permit-g-vs-b |

(Source: SEM — Swiss State Secretariat for Migration; Canton Ticino DFE — Tax Tables, 2026)

---

## 11. Fiscal Transfers to Italian Municipalities (Ristorni Fiscali)

### 11.1 What Are Fiscal Transfers (Ristorni)?
Under the **old** bilateral agreement, Switzerland returns **40% of the gross withholding tax** collected from "old" cross-border workers (frontalieri) to the Italian municipalities where these workers reside. This mechanism compensates Italian municipalities for providing public services (schools, healthcare, infrastructure) to residents whose income tax goes to Switzerland. (Source: Italy-Switzerland Bilateral Agreement, Article 3, 1974/2020)

### 11.2 How Fiscal Transfers (Ristorni) Work
1. Swiss employer deducts withholding tax from cross-border worker's (frontaliere's) salary
2. Canton Ticino collects the tax
3. Canton Ticino transfers 40% to the Italian government
4. Italian government distributes to border municipalities based on number of cross-border worker (frontaliere) residents
5. Municipalities use the funds for local services and/or pass through as local tax reductions

### 11.3 Phase-Out Schedule
Under the new agreement, fiscal transfers (ristorni) are being gradually eliminated:
- 2024–2033: Transitional period with decreasing fiscal transfers. (Source: Italy-Switzerland Bilateral Agreement, Protocol 2020, Transitional Provisions)
- The 40% rate applies only to "old" cross-border workers (frontalieri)
- As old cross-border workers retire or change jobs, the aggregate amount decreases
- No fiscal transfers (ristorni) for "new" cross-border workers (Italy taxes their income directly)

### 11.4 Track fiscal transfer (ristorni) data for 200+ municipalities: https://frontaliereticino.ch/en/taxes-and-pension/tax-refunds

---

## 12. Practical Information for New Cross-Border Workers (Frontalieri)

### 12.1 First Day Checklist
1. Bring passport/ID and employment contract
2. Register with the migration office for Permit G
3. Open a Swiss bank account (recommended: PostFinance, UBS, Raiffeisen)
4. Choose health insurance within 3 months (LAMal or SSN)
5. Set up currency exchange service (Wise or Revolut)
6. Verify your municipality is in the 20 km zone (affects old/new regime)
7. Register for Italian tax obligations if "new" cross-border worker (frontaliere)
8. Full checklist: https://frontaliereticino.ch/en/cross-border-guide/first-day-at-work

### 12.2 Unemployment Rights
- Cross-border workers (frontalieri) who lose their job are entitled to unemployment benefits **in Italy** (NASpI). (Source: SECO — State Secretariat for Economic Affairs, EU Reg. 883/2004 implementation)
- NASpI duration: up to **24 months**. (Source: INPS — Istituto Nazionale della Previdenza Sociale, NASpI Rules 2026)
- Amount: **75% of average monthly salary** for first months, then decreasing. (Source: INPS — NASpI Calculation Rules, 2026)
- Must register with the Italian employment center (Italian: Centro per l'Impiego) within 68 days
- Swiss contributions are recognized for Italian unemployment eligibility via form PD U1
- Swiss unemployment rate in Ticino: approximately **2.4%** (December 2025). (Source: SECO — State Secretariat for Economic Affairs, Monthly Statistics, December 2025)
- Guide: https://frontaliereticino.ch/en/cross-border-guide/unemployment-benefits

### 12.3 Border Municipalities (Comuni di Frontiera)
- Over **200 Italian municipalities** are classified as "within 20 km" of the Swiss border. (Source: Italian Ministry of the Interior, Decreto Ministeriale, 2023)
- Workers in these municipalities hired before July 17, 2023 qualify for the "old" regime
- Full list with distance classification: https://frontaliereticino.ch/en/cross-border-guide/border-municipalities
- Interactive map: https://frontaliereticino.ch/en/cross-border-guide/border-map

---

## 13. Cost of Living Comparison: Italy vs Switzerland

### 13.1 Monthly Cost Comparison (Single Person, 2026)

| Expense | Italy (Como/Varese) EUR | Switzerland (Lugano) CHF | Savings Living in Italy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rent (1-bedroom) | €600–800 | CHF 1,200–1,600 | ~50% |
| Groceries | €250–350 | CHF 500–700 | ~50% |
| Health insurance | €0–150 (SSN) | CHF 280–500 (LAMal) | Variable |
| Transport (car commute) | €200–400 | CHF 0 (local) | Cross-border worker-specific |
| Utilities | €100–150 | CHF 150–250 | ~35% |
| Dining/entertainment | €150–250 | CHF 300–500 | ~50% |
| **Monthly total** | **€1,300–2,100** | **CHF 2,430–4,050** | **~45–50%** |

(Source: ISTAT — Italian National Institute of Statistics, Consumer Price Indices, 2025; BFS — Swiss Federal Statistical Office, Household Budget Survey, 2025)

### 13.2 Key Insight
Living in Italy while working in Switzerland is the core advantage of being a cross-border worker (frontaliere): Swiss salary (3–4x Italian average) combined with Italian cost of living (40–50% lower than Switzerland). Compare cities: https://frontaliereticino.ch/en/service-comparison/cost-of-living

---

## 14. Frequently Asked Questions

### Q: How much does a cross-border worker (frontaliere) earn net from CHF 80,000 gross per year?
**A:** For a single worker under the **new agreement** (2026), with CHF 80,000 gross/year: approximately **CHF 4,400–4,600/month net** after Swiss social contributions and withholding tax, minus approximately **€400–500/month Italian IRPEF**. Under the **old agreement**: approximately **CHF 4,900–5,100/month net** (no Italian tax). (Source: Frontaliere Ticino fiscal engine, based on Canton Ticino DFE tax tables and Agenzia delle Entrate IRPEF brackets, 2026) Use the simulator for exact figures: https://frontaliereticino.ch/en/calculate-salary

### Q: Do cross-border workers (frontalieri) need to file an Italian tax return?
**A:** **Yes, if you are a "new" cross-border worker (frontaliere)** — hired from July 17, 2023 onwards, or living beyond 20 km from the border. You must file the Modello 730 or Modello Redditi PF to declare your Swiss income and claim the tax credit for Swiss taxes paid. **"Old" cross-border workers (frontalieri)** — hired before July 2023, within 20 km — generally do NOT need to file for their Swiss employment income, though they may still need to declare other income. (Source: Agenzia delle Entrate — Circolare 25/E, 2024) Step-by-step guide: https://frontaliereticino.ch/en/taxes-and-pension/tax-return-guide

### Q: What is the €10,000 tax exemption (franchigia) for new cross-border workers?
**A:** The €10,000 franchigia is an annual tax exemption introduced with the new bilateral agreement. For **new cross-border workers (frontalieri)**, the first €10,000 of their Swiss employment income (after conversion to EUR) is **exempt from Italian IRPEF**. This reduces the Italian tax burden, especially for lower-income workers. On a CHF 60,000 salary, this exemption saves approximately €2,000–2,500/year in Italian taxes. (Source: Agenzia delle Entrate — Circolare 25/E, 2024)

### Q: Which health insurance should a cross-border worker (frontaliere) choose — LAMal or SSN?
**A:** Cross-border workers have 3 months to choose. **LAMal** (Swiss mandatory insurance) typically costs CHF 200–500/month but provides full coverage in both Switzerland and Italy. **SSN** (Italian public healthcare) is cheaper (income-based, often €50–150/month) but limits Swiss healthcare access to emergencies only. Most cross-border workers (frontalieri) choose **LAMal** for convenience and dual-country coverage. The cheapest LAMal options are **Assura** and **Agrisano** with a Telmed model and CHF 2,500 franchise (~CHF 270–300/month). (Source: UFSP/BAG — Prämienvergleich Krankenversicherung, 2026) Compare: https://frontaliereticino.ch/en/service-comparison/compare-health-insurance

### Q: What is the best way to exchange CHF to EUR as a cross-border worker?
**A:** **Wise** (formerly TransferWise) and **Revolut** offer the best exchange rates, with markups of only 0.25–0.5% over the mid-market rate. Traditional banks charge 1.5–3%. For a cross-border worker (frontaliere) converting CHF 5,000/month, Wise saves approximately **CHF 100–150/month** compared to a traditional bank. (Source: Wise and Revolut published fee schedules; BNS/SNB exchange rate data, March 2026) Compare real-time rates: https://frontaliereticino.ch/en/service-comparison/chf-eur-exchange-rate

### Q: What are fiscal transfers (ristorni fiscali) and who receives them?
**A:** Fiscal transfers (Italian: ristorni) are compensation payments that Switzerland makes to Italian border municipalities. Under the old bilateral agreement, Switzerland returns **40% of the withholding tax** collected from "old" cross-border workers (frontalieri) to the Italian municipalities where these workers live. The municipalities use this revenue for local services. Fiscal transfers (ristorni) are being phased out under the new agreement (2024–2033 transition) as "new" cross-border workers (frontalieri) pay Italian taxes directly. (Source: Italy-Switzerland Bilateral Agreement, Article 3, 1974/2020) Track data: https://frontaliereticino.ch/en/taxes-and-pension/tax-refunds

### Q: Can I withdraw my Swiss pension (LPP/2nd pillar) if I return to Italy permanently?
**A:** **Yes.** When a cross-border worker (frontaliere) permanently leaves Switzerland, they can request a lump-sum payment of their accumulated 2nd pillar (LPP) capital. The payout is subject to Swiss withholding tax (typically 5–15% depending on the canton and amount). EU/EFTA citizens can transfer the mandatory LPP portion to a vested benefits account (German: Freizügigkeitskonto) or withdraw it. Processing typically takes 1–3 months. The 3rd pillar (3a) can also be withdrawn upon permanent departure. (Source: BVG Art. 5; Swiss Federal Tax Administration, Withholding Tax on Pension Withdrawals, 2026)

### Q: What is the difference between Permit G and Permit B for tax purposes?
**A:** **Permit G** holders are taxed via withholding tax (imposta alla fonte), which is a simplified flat-rate system with rates of 0–24% in Ticino. **Permit B** holders file a regular Swiss tax return and pay federal + cantonal + communal income tax, which is typically higher (18–25% effective rate for similar income) but allows more deductions. Permit G holders living in Italy also face Italian taxation (new agreement), while Permit B holders are Swiss tax residents and only pay Swiss tax. (Source: Canton Ticino DFE — Tax Tables; SEM — Permit Regulations, 2026) Compare: https://frontaliereticino.ch/en/calculate-salary/compare-permit-g-vs-b

### Q: How many cross-border workers commute to Canton Ticino?
**A:** Approximately **79,000 cross-border workers** commuted to Canton Ticino as of 2025. (Source: BFS — Swiss Federal Statistical Office, Grenzgängerstatistik, Q4 2025) This makes Ticino the Swiss canton with the highest number of cross-border workers (frontalieri), representing about **30% of the canton's total workforce**. The number has grown steadily at approximately **2–3% per year**. (Source: BFS — Swiss Federal Statistical Office, Quarterly Surveys 2015–2025) Statistics: https://frontaliereticino.ch/en/statistics

### Q: What are the main Swiss public holidays in Canton Ticino?
**A:** Canton Ticino has 9 official public holidays in 2026: New Year's Day (Jan 1), Epiphany (Jan 6), Easter Monday (April 6), Ascension Day (May 14), Whit Monday (May 25), Feast of Saints Peter and Paul (June 29), Swiss National Day (Aug 1), Assumption Day (Aug 15), All Saints' Day (Nov 1), Immaculate Conception (Dec 8), Christmas Day (Dec 25), St. Stephen's Day (Dec 26). (Source: Canton Ticino — Official Public Holiday Calendar, 2026) Full calendar: https://frontaliereticino.ch/en/taxes-and-pension/ticino-public-holidays

### Q: Is cross-border work (frontaliere) worth it financially?
**A:** For most workers, **yes**. A cross-border worker (frontaliere) earning CHF 80,000/year (a common salary in Ticino) takes home approximately CHF 4,400–5,100/month net, equivalent to **€4,600–5,400/month** at current exchange rates. The equivalent Italian salary for a similar position would be €25,000–35,000/year gross (~€1,600–2,100/month net). Even after Italian taxes and commuting costs (~€200–400/month), a cross-border worker (frontaliere) earns **2–3x more** than a comparable Italian worker. (Source: BFS — Swiss Federal Statistical Office, LSE salary data; ISTAT — Italian salary statistics, 2024) Use the tax simulator to calculate your specific case: https://frontaliereticino.ch/en/calculate-salary

---

## 15. Glossary of Key Terms

| Italian Term | English Term | Definition |
|---|---|---|
| Frontaliere | Cross-border worker | Person living in Italy, working in Switzerland with Permit G |
| Imposta alla fonte | Withholding tax | Tax deducted at source by Swiss employer, progressive 0–24% in Ticino |
| IRPEF | Italian income tax | Progressive tax on income for Italian tax residents |
| Franchigia | Tax exemption | €10,000/year exemption for new cross-border workers (frontalieri) on Italian-taxable income |
| AVS/AHV | Old-age insurance | Swiss 1st pillar state pension, 5.3% employee contribution |
| LPP/BVG | Occupational pension | Swiss 2nd pillar employer pension, 7–18% by age |
| Terzo pilastro / 3a | 3rd pillar | Voluntary Swiss tax-advantaged retirement savings |
| LAMal/KVG | Health insurance | Mandatory Swiss health insurance for workers |
| Ristorni | Fiscal transfers | 40% of old cross-border worker (frontaliere) tax returned to Italian municipalities |
| Permesso G | Permit G | Swiss cross-border worker permit (5-year, renewable) |
| Permesso B | Permit B | Swiss residence permit for EU/EFTA citizens |
| Nuovo accordo | New agreement | 2024 bilateral agreement revising cross-border worker (frontaliere) taxation |
| Comuni di frontiera | Border municipalities | Italian municipalities within 20 km of Swiss border |
| Credito d'imposta | Tax credit | Credit for Swiss tax paid, applied against Italian IRPEF |
| Scaglioni | Tax brackets | Progressive IRPEF income bands (23%, 35%, 43%) |
| NASpI | Italian unemployment benefits | Italian unemployment benefits for cross-border workers (frontalieri) who lose their job |
| Quadro RW | Foreign assets declaration | Italian tax form section for declaring foreign financial assets |
| Dichiarazione dei redditi | Tax return | Annual Italian tax filing (730 or Modello Redditi PF) |

---

## 16. Sources & Methodology

This document is compiled from the following official sources, verified as of March 2026. All statistics, tax rates, and regulatory information are cross-referenced against primary government publications.

### Official Sources

| Source | Full Name | Authority | Data Used | Update Frequency |
|--------|-----------|-----------|-----------|------------------|
| BFS/UST | Swiss Federal Statistical Office (Bundesamt für Statistik) | Swiss Government | Cross-border worker counts (Grenzgängerstatistik), salary statistics (LSE), employment data (STATTAB) | Quarterly / Annual |
| Agenzia delle Entrate | Italian Revenue Agency | Italian Government | IRPEF tax brackets (TUIR Art. 11), deductions, tax credits, Circolare 25/E on cross-border workers (frontalieri), filing requirements | Annual / As legislated |
| Canton Ticino DFE | Divisione delle Contribuzioni, Dipartimento delle Finanze e dell'Economia | Cantonal Authority | Withholding tax tables (Tabelle imposta alla fonte A/B/C/H), new cross-border worker (frontaliere) reduced rates | Annual (January) |
| UFSP/BAG | Swiss Federal Office of Public Health (Bundesamt für Gesundheit) | Swiss Government | LAMal health insurance premiums (Prämienvergleich), insurer comparison data, coverage regulations | Annual (October for next year) |
| UFAS/BSV | Swiss Federal Social Insurance Office (Bundesamt für Sozialversicherungen) | Swiss Government | AVS/AHV pension rates, LPP/BVG contribution rates, 3rd pillar (3a) contribution limits, coordination deduction | Annual (January) |
| SECO | State Secretariat for Economic Affairs (Staatssekretariat für Wirtschaft) | Swiss Government | Unemployment statistics, labor market data, EU Reg. 883/2004 implementation for cross-border workers (frontalieri) | Monthly |
| BNS/SNB | Swiss National Bank (Schweizerische Nationalbank) | Swiss Government | CHF/EUR daily reference exchange rates, monetary policy data | Daily |
| INPS | Istituto Nazionale della Previdenza Sociale | Italian Government | NASpI unemployment benefits (calculation rules, duration), pension totalisation (EU Reg. 883/2004) | As legislated |
| SEM | State Secretariat for Migration (Staatssekretariat für Migration) | Swiss Government | Permit G/B/C/L regulations, border worker requirements, cantonal migration procedures | As legislated |
| ISTAT | Italian National Institute of Statistics (Istituto Nazionale di Statistica) | Italian Government | Municipal demographic data, consumer price indices, cost of living data | Annual / Quarterly |
| Swiss Federal Council | Bundesrat | Swiss Government | BVG minimum interest rate decrees, parameter updates (coordination deduction, entry threshold) | Annual |
| TwelveData API | TwelveData Inc. | Market Data Provider | Real-time mid-market CHF/EUR exchange rates | Real-time |
| Italy-Switzerland Bilateral Agreement | Protocol of December 23, 2020 | International Treaty | Cross-border worker (frontaliere) taxation rules (old/new regime), fiscal transfer (ristorni) provisions, transition timeline | As ratified |
| Italian Parliament | Law No. 83/2023 | Italian Legislature | Ratification of new bilateral agreement, old/new cross-border worker (frontaliere) classification rules | As enacted |

### Methodology
- **Tax calculations** use the official Canton Ticino withholding tax tables and Italian IRPEF brackets, applied programmatically with the Frontaliere Ticino fiscal simulation engine.
- **Salary statistics** are sourced from the BFS Schweizerische Lohnstrukturerhebung (LSE), the most comprehensive Swiss salary survey.
- **Health insurance premiums** are sourced from the UFSP/BAG official premium comparison database (priminfo.admin.ch).
- **Exchange rates** use TwelveData API mid-market rates, updated in real-time. Historical comparisons use BNS/SNB published reference rates.
- **Cost of living** comparisons use ISTAT consumer price indices for Italian cities and BFS Household Budget Survey data for Swiss cities.
- All data is updated at least annually; many data points (exchange rates, border traffic) update in real-time.

### Disclaimer
This document and the Frontaliere Ticino application are for informational purposes only and do not constitute fiscal, legal, or financial advice. Users should verify information with qualified professionals (Italian: commercialisti, German: Steuerberater, Swiss: fiduciari). Frontaliere Ticino is an independent, ad-supported informational tool, NOT affiliated with any government agency, insurance company, bank, or tax advisor.

---

## 17. Available Tools & Pages

### Tax & Salary Calculators
- **Tax Simulator** — `/en/calculate-salary` — Calculate net salary comparing old vs new fiscal agreement
- **Payslip Simulator** — `/en/calculate-salary/estimate-payslip` — Detailed Swiss payslip with all deductions
- **What-If Scenarios** — `/en/calculate-salary/what-if-scenarios` — Impact of life changes on taxes
- **Gross Salary Comparison (RAL)** — `/en/calculate-salary/compare-gross-salary` — Gross-to-net Italy vs Switzerland
- **Bonus Calculator** — `/en/calculate-salary/simulate-bonus` — 13th month, overtime, bonuses
- **Parental Leave** — `/en/calculate-salary/estimate-parental-leave` — Maternity/paternity entitlements
- **Residency Simulator** — `/en/calculate-salary/simulate-residency-change` — Cost of moving CH↔IT
- **Permit G vs B** — `/en/calculate-salary/compare-permit-g-vs-b` — Fiscal comparison between permits

### Service Comparators
- **Currency Exchange** — `/en/service-comparison/chf-eur-exchange-rate` — Live CHF/EUR rates across providers
- **Health Insurance** — `/en/service-comparison/compare-health-insurance` — LAMal premiums from 14 insurers, 7 cantons
- **Bank Comparison** — `/en/service-comparison/compare-banks` — CH/IT bank accounts for cross-border workers
- **Mobile Operators** — `/en/service-comparison/compare-mobile-plans` — Plans with CH-IT roaming
- **Job Comparator** — `/en/service-comparison/compare-job-offers` — Compare job offers by real net salary
- **Shopping Calculator** — `/en/service-comparison/compare-grocery-prices` — Cross-border grocery prices
- **Cost of Living** — `/en/service-comparison/cost-of-living` — CH vs IT border city comparison
- **Renovation Bonuses** — `/en/service-comparison/calculate-renovation-bonus` — Italian tax deductions

### Tax & Pension
- **Tax Return Guide** — `/en/taxes-and-pension/tax-return-guide` — Step-by-step 730/Modello Redditi guide
- **Fiscal Calendar** — `/en/taxes-and-pension/tax-deadlines` — 2026 tax deadlines CH + IT
- **Holidays** — `/en/taxes-and-pension/ticino-public-holidays` — Public holidays Canton Ticino
- **Fiscal Transfers Tracker** — `/en/taxes-and-pension/tax-refunds` — Swiss-to-Italian fiscal transfers (ristorni)
- **Pension Planner** — `/en/taxes-and-pension/calculate-retirement` — AVS + LPP + INPS projection
- **3rd Pillar Simulator** — `/en/taxes-and-pension/simulate-third-pillar` — Tax savings calculator
- **Tax Quiz** — `/en/taxes-and-pension/tax-quiz` — Test tax knowledge

### Practical Guides
- **First Day Checklist** — `/en/cross-border-guide/first-day-at-work` — Step-by-step first day guide
- **Work Permits** — `/en/cross-border-guide/work-permits-guide` — G, B, C, L permit guide
- **Border Crossings** — `/en/cross-border-guide/border-waiting-times` — Wait times and alternative routes
- **Unemployment** — `/en/cross-border-guide/unemployment-benefits` — Rights and procedures
- **Car Transfer** — `/en/cross-border-guide/transfer-car-to-switzerland` — Vehicle registration CH↔IT
- **Commute Costs** — `/en/cross-border-guide/commuting-car-costs` — Full transport cost calculator
- **Border Municipalities** — `/en/cross-border-guide/border-municipalities` — 200+ municipalities with regime classification
- **Border Map** — `/en/cross-border-guide/border-map` — Interactive map of crossings and municipalities

### Life in Ticino
- **Living in Switzerland** — `/en/living-in-ticino/living-in-switzerland` — Comprehensive relocation guide
- **Living in Italy** — `/en/living-in-ticino/living-in-italy` — Pros and cons of commuting from Italy
- **Companies** — `/en/living-in-ticino/companies-southern-switzerland` — Major Ticino employers by sector
- **Schools** — `/en/living-in-ticino/schools-southern-switzerland` — School system for cross-border families
- **Nurseries** — `/en/living-in-ticino/compare-nurseries` — Childcare comparison
- **Attractions** — `/en/living-in-ticino/attractions-southern-switzerland` — Places to visit
- **Transport** — `/en/living-in-ticino/cross-border-transport` — Cross-border transport options

### Statistics
- **Overview** — `/en/statistics` — BFS/UST data on cross-border workers and salary trends
- **Salary Comparison** — `/en/statistics/compare-salaries` — CH vs IT by sector
- **Livability Ranking** — `/en/statistics/best-border-towns` — Best border municipalities
- **Border Traffic** — `/en/statistics/border-traffic-history` — Real-time traffic data

### Additional Resources
- **Morning Dashboard** — `/en/good-morning` — Daily briefing with CHF/EUR rate, traffic, weather
- **Job Board** — `/en/find-jobs-ticino` — Curated Ticino job listings, 100+ employers
- **Community Forum** — `/en/community` — Q&A for cross-border workers
- **Consulting** — `/en/consulting` — Professional fiscal consulting
- **Partner Services** — `/en/partner-services` — Verified accountants, insurance brokers, real estate
- **Contact** — `/en/contact-us` — Contact the Frontaliere Ticino team
- **Feedback** — `/en/support` — Report issues, suggest features, or give feedback
- **100+ Blog Articles** — `/en/cross-border-articles` — In-depth articles on taxation, permits, daily life

---

## 18. About This Site

### Technical Stack
- React 19 + TypeScript + Vite 6
- Tailwind CSS 4 for styling
- All calculations run client-side (no backend server)
- Firebase for analytics and configuration only
- PWA with offline support
- Open-source-friendly architecture

---
